titleStmt.title: 321. Memorandum From Robert W. Komer of the National Security Council Staff to President of the National Security Council Staff to President Johnson

div.head: 321. Memorandum From Robert W. Komer of the National Security Council Staff to President Johnson Source: Johnson Library, National Security File, Country File, Yemen, Memos, Vol. I, 11/63-6/64. Secret.
div.p: Yemen. This is merely to inform you that we have quietly and successfully redeployed our miniscule jet fighter force (8 planes) out of Saudi Arabia.On June 13, 1963, President Kennedy approved the temporary deployment of an air unit (operation Hard Surface) composed of eight U.S. F-100D tactical fighter aircraft and one transport-type command support aircraft to Saudi Arabia in exchange for Saudi agreement to end all aid to the Royalist side in the Yemen civil war. Defense has been panting to do so for months and you’ll recall we finally agreed to leave them there till 31 January only to make sure Faysal extended the disengagement rather than reopening the Yemen war.

Your 19 January letter to FaysalReference is to Johnson’s letter of December 19, 1963; see Foreign Relations, 1961–1963, vol. XVIII, Document 389. clinched his adherence, and he raised no objection at all when we told him we were withdrawing above “training” mission. His subsequent friendly letter to youFor text of Faisal’s January 11 letter, see Document 221. didn’t even raise the question (we’re preparing a suitable reply).

In fact, our best guess is that the Yemen flap is about over (there’s been little fighting for months), and a UAR/Saudi reconciliation in the cards. I may be wrong but I think we can keep this messy little problem off your list of trouble spots.

signed.note: Printed from a copy that bears this typed signature.
